risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We used permuted block randomization at the level of the patient. Once eligibility was determined, written consent obtained, and the baseline interview completed, the study coordinator opened a sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment." Comment: The sequence was explicitly described as computer-generated random assignment with permuted blocks, which is a truly random allocation method and meets criteria for low risk.

Quote: "Once eligibility was determined, written consent obtained, and the baseline interview completed, the study coordinator opened a sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment." Comment: The use of a sealed envelope opened only after eligibility, consent, and baseline assessment indicates the assignment was not foreseeable before enrollment, which is an explicit concealment mechanism.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Clinical and study staff and study participants were all blinded to assignment until after the baseline interview. The nature of the intervention meant that participants were not blind to the condition after participation in the intervention." Comment: Although blinding was maintained only until after baseline, the intervention was inherently unblinded during delivery (decision aid/video and motivational interviewing vs attention control booklet). The main outcomes included patient willingness, knowledge, expectations, and discussion/referral outcomes, all of which could plausibly be influenced by participants and personnel knowing the assigned intervention, so the dominant risk is high.

Quote: "Clinical and study staff and study participants were all blinded to assignment until after the baseline interview. The nature of the intervention meant that participants were not blind to the condition after participation in the intervention." and "Participant interviews were completed at baseline and at 1, 3 & 12 months following the intervention... The willingness rating is a 5-category ordinal response scale... Referral to orthopedic clinic, seeing an orthopedic surgeon, and the receipt of TKR were all assessed only at 12 months after the intervention. This was done by telephone survey and validated by chart review." Comment: The outcomes were mainly patient self-report via telephone interviews, so the patient was effectively the assessor for willingness, discussion with PCP, and some referral/attendance outcomes. The report does not clearly state that outcome assessment was blinded after the intervention, and participants were explicitly not blind after receiving the intervention, so detection bias for these subjective/self-reported outcomes cannot be ruled out. Although referral/TKR were validated by chart review, the paper does not specify that chart abstractors were blinded, and the outcome set includes a mix of self-reported and record-validated measures; therefore the blinding of outcome assessment remains unclear.

Quote: "There were no losses to follow-up except for one patient in the MI arm and one patient in DA/MI arm. Over the course of the study 93% of the subjects completed at least 2 of the 3 follow-up interviews with no differences among the 4 intervention groups (p=0.62)." Comment: Attrition was very low overall and balanced across groups, with no indication that missing data were related to outcome or intervention assignment. The small amount of missing follow-up is unlikely to have biased the results, so this domain is Low risk.
